summary: I'm Edward Roberts, a physician who has practiced medicine in Mount Sterling since 1979. I helped found the Post Clinic in the 1990s and have served as its board chairman and medical director. I volunteered at the clinic until late 2024, after which I stepped aside from clinical duties but remain involved in leadership. My journey into medicine was inspired by my father, who was also a physician.

Mount Sterling is my hometown where I've practiced medicine since 1979. It's a small community where I know many people personally, which makes my work rewarding yet sometimes challenging. The Post Clinic, which I helped start, operates here to provide free healthcare to those in need. The old post office building on Main Street, near the Presbyterian Church, was an important early site for the clinic.

My father worked for Inland Steel Company as a physician in Wheelwright, Kentucky, a coal mining town, before moving to Central Kentucky in the 1950s. His work there influenced my view of medicine and community health. Seeing his role in that industrial setting helped shape my understanding of the challenges in providing healthcare to working-class populations.

Wheelwright is a coal mining town in Eastern Kentucky where my father practiced medicine for Inland Steel Company before relocating to Central Kentucky. His experience there was part of my early exposure to the medical profession. Although I never practiced there myself, Wheelwright represents the roots of my family's medical legacy.

During my undergraduate years, the Vietnam War was a time of great turbulence and protest, which I participated in. Initially, I intended to pursue law and politics, but the war and the social unrest made me reconsider my path. I decided to become a physician to do something useful and meaningful instead.

entity: University of Kentucky College of Medicine
summary: I attended the University of Kentucky College of Medicine and graduated in 1976. This was where I formally trained to become a physician after shifting my focus from law to medicine. The education I received there prepared me for my career in internal medicine and community healthcare in Kentucky.

I had considered going to Danville, Kentucky, to practice internal medicine away from my family, but was persuaded that they didn't need extra doctors. So instead, I returned to Mount Sterling, where my family was based, to begin my medical career. Danville was part of my early career considerations but not where I ultimately settled.

Kroger is a local store in Mount Sterling where I sometimes find myself discussing medications with neighbors because I can't always remember the names of the little white pills. It’s a small-town experience that highlights how familiar we all are with each other. This casual interaction reflects the close-knit community I serve as a physician.

I was involved in forming the Montgomery County Medical Society in the 1980s to address the lack of affordable healthcare access. The society started mostly as a social group but evolved into a platform to discuss and act on community health needs. It was instrumental in founding the Post Clinic to provide free primary care to those who couldn't afford it.

The Presbyterian Church in Mount Sterling played a crucial role in the founding of the Post Clinic by providing an empty building next to the church on Main Street. They supported the clinic by paying for janitorial services, utilities, and insurance. Many church members volunteered at the clinic, especially in patient check-in roles, making it a true community partnership.

Main Street in Mount Sterling is where the Presbyterian Church had an empty building that became the original site of the Post Clinic in the 1990s. This location was central to the clinic’s early operations and community presence. It’s also where the old post office building, which inspired the clinic’s name, was located.

I helped found the Post Clinic around 1996 to provide free primary care to those in Montgomery County who couldn't afford it. We started in a building next to the Presbyterian Church and later expanded to include a dental clinic. Over 30 years, the clinic has grown to serve thousands of patients with a mix of volunteers and a small paid staff, relying on community donations and some local government support.

Dr. Tingle was our dental director who approached us about setting up a dental clinic under the Post Clinic umbrella. He found used dental equipment and helped establish the dental services in a building provided by Dr. Razor. His leadership was key in expanding the clinic’s services beyond medical care.

Dr. Razor owned a dental building with a vacant office that he generously allowed us to use for the Post Clinic’s dental services. This helped us establish a dental clinic separate from the medical clinic initially. His support was essential in the early development of dental care for underserved patients.

Bob Klink was our clinic director for many years and was a retired social worker who helped manage the Post Clinic’s operations. He provided steady leadership until Louise Summers took over about 15 to 20 years ago. His work was foundational in keeping the clinic running smoothly during its early decades.

Louise Summers took over as clinic director from Bob Klink about 15 to 20 years ago and has been a part-time employee since then. She helps manage the Post Clinic’s daily operations and is supported by a small paid staff including a dentist and nurse practitioner. Louise’s dedication has been vital to the clinic’s ongoing success.

We sought federal grants to build a new, fully accessible clinic building after realizing the old one was not handicap accessible. With the help of local and state officials, we secured two federal grants to fund construction. Although the clinic operates mostly without federal money, these grants were crucial for improving accessibility and infrastructure.

The old post office building on Main Street in Mount Sterling was built in the 1920s and is about 100 years old. It has a beautiful granite foundation and a stained glass skylight inside. The building was initially considered for parking by the Presbyterian Church when the post office moved out, but its name inspired the Post Clinic’s name.
